one_line_takeaway: Across 14,664 experiments on DRL agents, six standard plasticity interventions (Shrink & Perturb, Weight Clipping, Spectral Norm, Weight Decay, Layer Norm, ReDo) incidentally mitigate backdoor attacks, but Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM) incidentally strengthens them -- raising attack success rate from 0.178 to 0.326 in TM-Post robotic tasks -- by flattening the backdoor's loss landscape, and combining SAM with other interventions amplifies this effect further (ASR up to 0.418).
key_finding: In TM-Scratch, interventions have only a modest effect on backdoors (largest ASR shift: Layer Normalization, -8.84%; largest BTP degradation: Layer Normalization reducing BTP by over 30% on Acrobot/MountainCar). In TM-Post, effects are much larger: Weight Clipping reduces ASR by an average of 17.46% and Spectral Normalization by 11.78%; Weight Clipping reduces BTP by an average of 20.19% and Layer Normalization by 11.93%. SAM is the sole exception -- in TM-Post robotic tasks it raises ASR from 0.178 to 0.326 (an 83.15% relative increase) while simultaneously raising BTP from 0.745 to 0.814; in physics-control tasks the ASR gain is 26.07%. Backdoor attacks themselves inflate loss-landscape-sharpness range by 635.22% (vs. 98.63% for weight magnitude and 19.16% for effective rank) relative to conventional training, and SAM is the only intervention that suppresses this sharpness (down to an average of 2.11% of the unmitigated backdoored level), which the paper argues is mechanistically why SAM alone strengthens the attack. Combining interventions with SAM compounds the effect: on the three robotic tasks, going from SAM alone to Plastic/SLac/SSW combinations pushes ASR from 0.178 to 0.368/0.417/0.418 and BTP from 0.745 to 0.724/0.816/0.915, correlating with the authors' Pathological Diagnosis distance metric (PD = 9.43, 17.42, 18.64 respectively).
core_contribution: An empirical study (ICML 2026, Zhejiang University et al.) that measures how seven mainstream 'plasticity interventions' used in deep reinforcement learning (DRL) — Shrink & Perturb, Weight Clipping, Spectral Normalization, Weight Decay, Layer Normalization, ReDo, and SAM — interact with backdoor attacks on DRL agents. The paper runs 14,664 experimental cases (9,024 for single interventions across two threat models, plus 5,640 for five intervention combinations) and finds that six of the seven interventions mitigate backdoor attacks to varying degrees while only SAM (Sharpness-Aware Minimization) exacerbates them. It then attributes these effects to three mechanisms (activation pathway disruption, representation space compression, backdoor gradient amplification) and proposes two forward-looking, conceptual (not empirically validated as new attacks/defenses) contributions: a framework called Sweeper-Converter-Connector (SCC) for reasoning about/deliberately composing interventions to make backdoor injection more robust, and the observation that abnormal loss-landscape sharpness is a candidate signal for backdoor detection.
threat_model: The paper adopts DRL backdoor threat models from prior work (TrojDRL/Kiourti et al. 2020, BadRL/Cui et al. 2024, SleeperNets/Rathbun et al. 2024, UNIDOOR/Ma et al. 2025), not a new one of its own. Two variants are studied: TM-Scratch, where the adversary injects the backdoor while the provider trains the agent from scratch, and TM-Post, where the adversary downloads a released (already-trained) agent, injects a backdoor via post-training fine-tuning, and republishes it to a third-party platform. In both, the backdoored agent behaves normally on the benign task until the adversary inserts a trigger into the environment, at which point the policy is forced toward a predefined target action; the adversary's stated objective (Sec. 3) is to minimize the distance between the policy's output and the target action under the trigger while preserving benign-task performance. The paper's own contribution is not a new attack or defense against this threat model, but an investigation of how plasticity interventions -- which providers add for unrelated reasons (mitigating plasticity loss during continual training) -- change the strength of this pre-existing threat.
limitations: The paper does not state these itself as a dedicated limitations section (none was found in the pages parsed), but grounded caveats are evident from the text: (1) SCC and sharpness-based detection are explicitly framed as 'conceptual framework' and a 'promising avenue' -- neither is implemented/evaluated as a standalone new attack or defense pipeline with its own success-rate numbers; the paper itself flags for sharpness-based detection that sharpness varies substantially across DRL tasks (complicating a unified threshold) and that other non-backdoor causes of abnormal sharpness are not yet disentangled from backdoor causes. (2) All backdoor attacks studied are from existing prior work (TrojDRL, BadRL, SleeperNets, UNIDOOR) via transition-tampering-style injection; the study does not test environment-perturbation or policy-combination injection techniques it cites in Background. (3) Findings are specific to the seven interventions and combinations tested (single-agent actor-critic-style architectures on 12 relatively small/simulated control tasks); generalization to other DRL architectures (e.g., large multi-agent or offline RL systems) is not evaluated in the excerpted text.

datasets_used: OpenAI Gym classic control: CartPole, Acrobot, MountainCar, Pendulum
OpenAI Gym physics control: Lunar Lander, BipedalWalker
PyBullet robotic tasks: Hopper, Reacher, Half Cheetah
47 constructed backdoor tasks (built following Ma et al. 2025), single- and multi-backdoor settings
quantitative_results: TM-Post, robotic tasks: SAM raises ASR from 0.178 to 0.326 (83.15% relative increase) and BTP from 0.745 to 0.814
TM-Post, physics-control tasks: SAM's ASR gain is 26.07%
TM-Post: Weight Clipping reduces ASR by an average of 17.46%, Spectral Normalization by 11.78%
TM-Post: Weight Clipping reduces BTP by an average of 20.19%, Layer Normalization by 11.93%
TM-Scratch: Layer Normalization produces the largest ASR change, -8.84%, and reduces BTP by over 30% on Acrobot and MountainCar
Backdoor attacks (vs. conventional training) increase the range of weight magnitude by 98.63%, effective rank by 19.16%, and loss-landscape sharpness by 635.22%
SAM compresses backdoor-induced loss-landscape sharpness to an average of 2.11% of its unmitigated level
Combination study (three robotic tasks): ASR rises from 0.178 (None) to 0.368 (Plastic), 0.417 (SLac), 0.418 (SSW); BTP rises from 0.745 to 0.724, 0.816, 0.915 respectively
Pathological Diagnosis (PD) values: Swiss Cheese = 0.52, Plastic = 9.43, SLac = 17.42, SSW = 18.64

Normalized-dot-product gradient alignment: without intervention the backdoor state's gradient direction is near 0 (orthogonal) relative to benign states; with Spectral Normalization it approaches 1.00 (aligned)

Average pathological rankings (1-8 scale, lower/extreme = more distinctive) reported in Figure 5, e.g. Weight Clipping weight-magnitude ranking v31 = lowest among interventions; Spectral Normalization effective-rank ranking v42 = 1.81 (best); SAM sharpness ranking v83 = 2.72 (lowest sharpness)
baselines_compared: None (no intervention) as the reference condition
Single interventions: Shrink & Perturb, Weight Clipping, Spectral Normalization, Weight Decay, Layer Normalization, ReDo, SAM
Existing combinations: Swiss Cheese (Lyle et al. 2024), Plastic (Lee et al. 2023)
New combinations constructed by the authors: Lac, SLac, SSW
Backdoor attack algorithms used as the attack substrate (not baselines being compared against each other, but the injection methods evaluated): TrojDRL, BadRL, SleeperNets, UNIDOOR
